title: UAE Exits OPEC as Gulf States Hedge Against End of Oil Age
subhead: The UAE's departure from OPEC after nearly 60 years signals a strategic pivot to maximize revenues before demand peaks, while Venezuela emerges as India's third-largest supplier and Ukraine's drone campaign reaches deep into Russian refining infrastructure.

sections.heading: UAE Leaves OPEC, Citing 'Autumn of the Hydrocarbon Age'
sections.body:
The United Arab Emirates has formally exited OPEC after nearly six decades, with a senior presidential adviser revealing the decision was three years in the making and driven by a conviction that the world is approaching the "autumn of the hydrocarbon age." Anwar Gargash, the UAE presidential adviser, said the Gulf state needs to maximize oil revenues while demand remains strong, rather than accept production quotas from a cartel whose influence is waning.

The UAE holds the world's seventh-largest proven oil reserves and has chafed under OPEC+ production limits for years. The departure reflects Abu Dhabi's calculation that constrained supply benefits competitors and that the energy transition timeline has accelerated. By breaking from OPEC, the UAE gains freedom to pump at capacity—potentially adding 1 million barrels per day to global supply—at a moment when prices remain elevated due to the Iran conflict.

The move sends a signal to other Gulf producers: the cartel model that defined oil geopolitics for decades faces structural pressure from both the energy transition and internal competition. Saudi Arabia, OPEC's dominant producer, now faces a more fragmented producer landscape just as it navigates the Hormuz crisis.
sections.whyItMatters: The fragmentation of OPEC weakens the cartel's ability to manage prices and creates uncertainty about future supply discipline—particularly significant as markets already roiled by the Iran conflict absorb the implications of a major producer going independent.

sections.heading: Venezuela Becomes India's Third-Largest Crude Supplier
sections.body:
Venezuelan crude shipments to India have surged nearly 50% in May, making the sanctions-hit South American nation India's third-largest oil supplier this month. The jump comes as India scrambles for alternatives to Middle Eastern crude after the Strait of Hormuz closure disrupted nearly half of its imports. Venezuelan shipments reached approximately 417,000 barrels per day in May, up from 283,000 bpd in April.

The shift is being facilitated by Washington's post-Maduro policy reversal. After US forces removed Nicolás Maduro in January, American authorities began authorizing direct purchases from PDVSA, Venezuela's state oil company. Venezuela holds 303 billion barrels of proven reserves—the world's largest—and Washington is now pushing to bring that supply online to offset Iranian crude.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio will travel to India May 23-26 alongside Venezuela's interim president Delcy Rodriguez to negotiate expanded energy sales. The parallel visits illustrate how the Iran conflict is reshuffling global energy relationships: India, which had grown dependent on Iranian and Russian crude, is being pushed toward Venezuelan supply as Washington tightens its grip on both Tehran's and Moscow's revenues.
sections.whyItMatters: The Venezuela-India supply chain represents a fundamental reordering of global oil flows—one that reduces Iranian leverage in peace negotiations while simultaneously advancing US strategic control over one of the world's largest reserves.

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes Russian Refineries Deep Inside Russian Territory
sections.body:
Ukrainian forces have struck the Yaroslavl oil refinery—a 300,000-barrel-per-day facility located 700 kilometers from Ukrainian territory—marking the fourth attack on that refinery in a single month. President Zelenskyy announced the strike as part of Ukraine's ongoing campaign to "bring the war back home to Russia." A second strike hit the Syzran refinery in Russia's Samara region hours earlier.

Ukraine has intensified attacks on Russian refining and export infrastructure as international oil prices surge. The strategy aims to reduce Russia's export revenues while Moscow benefits from elevated global prices caused by the Iran conflict. Russia has seen increased oil revenues as prices have jumped and US sanctions waivers have permitted continued Indian purchases of Russian crude already loaded on tankers.

The attacks demonstrate Ukraine's ability to project power deep into Russian territory and complicate Moscow's energy infrastructure. Russia's NORSI refinery—the country's fourth-largest—was also partially shut after a drone attack, reducing domestic refining capacity. Ukraine's drone campaign is targeting both export infrastructure and the internal fuel supply chain, forcing Russia to redirect military resources to infrastructure defense.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's long-range drone strikes create a new constraint on Russian refining capacity at a moment when Moscow is already navigating elevated oil prices and supply chain disruptions—potentially squeezing Russia's export revenues even as prices rise.

sections.heading: China's Rare Earth Export Squeeze on Japan Over Taiwan Tensions
sections.body:
China has blocked Japan's access to several heavy rare earth elements for at least four months, coinciding with heightened tensions over Taiwan. The export restrictions echo Beijing's 2010 rare earth embargo that targeted Japan during a territorial dispute, highlighting the strategic weaponization of critical mineral supply chains. Japan depends on China for approximately 60% of its rare earth imports, materials essential for electronics, electric vehicles, and defense applications.

The restrictions come amid broader US-China technology competition and China's efforts to leverage its dominance in critical minerals. China controls approximately 70% of global rare earth production and 90% of processing capacity. Heavy rare earths—including dysprosium and terbium, used in magnets for electric vehicles and wind turbines—are particularly concentrated in Chinese supply chains.

Japan has sought to diversify supply sources since 2010, investing in Australian, Vietnamese, and domestic production, but remains heavily dependent on Chinese processing. The current squeeze demonstrates China's willingness to use mineral supply chains as leverage in geopolitical disputes—a warning to other advanced economies about dependence on Chinese processing.
sections.whyItMatters: The rare earth restrictions signal Beijing's readiness to weaponize critical mineral supply chains in Taiwan-related confrontations, with implications for Japan's semiconductor industry and the broader Western effort to decouple from Chinese supply chains.

sections.heading: India's $9 Billion Nicobar Megaport Targets Malacca Chokepoint
sections.body:
India is constructing a $9 billion megaport at Great Nicobar Island in the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago—a strategic project that will allow New Delhi to monitor maritime traffic near the Strait of Malacca. The port, airport, and logistics hub spans 166 square kilometers and is slated for completion over three decades, with the first phase operational by 2028.

The project sharpens what Chinese strategists have long called the "Malacca dilemma"—China's vulnerability to a blockade of the narrow strait through which 80% of its energy imports flow. India's positioning at Great Nicobar, closer to Indonesia than mainland India, creates monitoring capability over one of the world's most critical maritime chokepoints.

The project also reduces India's dependence on other transshipment hubs like Singapore and Colombo. According to the Observer Research Foundation, approximately 60% of India's sea-based trade and significant volumes of LNG imports transit through the Malacca Strait. As the Foreign Affairs analysis of the Hormuz crisis noted, similar chokepoint dynamics could be replicated in Indo-Pacific waterways—a consideration driving Indian strategic investment.
sections.whyItMatters: India's Nicobar project represents long-term positioning to leverage the same chokepoint strategy Iran has employed at Hormuz—creating potential leverage over China's energy supply while reducing India's own maritime vulnerability.

sections.heading: Belarus Hostes Russian Nuclear Weapons Drills Amid Ukraine Tensions
sections.body:
Russia has deployed additional tactical nuclear warheads to Belarus for the first time during joint exercises that concluded Thursday. Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko participated alongside Vladimir Putin in the three-day drills, which included launches of the Yars intercontinental ballistic missile—capable of carrying three nuclear warheads—from northwestern Russia to the Pacific Kamchatka Peninsula.

The exercises involved hundreds of Russian missile launchers, warplanes, warships, and nuclear submarines across a theater spanning from Eastern Europe to the Pacific. Russia supplied Belarus with modified Su-25 fighter jets and Iskander-M ballistic missiles, with nuclear weapons reportedly stored at the Asipovichi military range, less than 200 kilometers from the Ukrainian border.

Ukrainian officials have warned the drills may signal preparations for a new offensive from Belarusian territory, though analysts note current Russian force concentrations in Belarus are insufficient for a major offensive. Lukashenko stated Belarus would not be "dragged into the war" but would "defend itself with Russia" if attacked—a calibrated position reflecting his balancing act between Moscow's demands and Belarusian economic pressures.
sections.whyItMatters: The nuclear deployment signals Moscow's willingness to raise escalation risks while Lukashenko navigates between Russian pressure and Western overtures—creating a new nuclear threat axis near Ukraine's northern border.

sections.heading: EU Warns of Stagflation as Second Energy Shock in Five Years
sections.body:
The European Commission is downgrading its growth forecast and raising inflation projections as the Iran conflict delivers a "stagflationary shock"—the second energy crisis in under five years. European Economic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis warned that policymakers have limited margin for fiscal response compared to the COVID pandemic, constraining the ability to cushion households from price spikes.

The International Energy Agency has cautioned that global oil inventories are depleting at record pace, with inventories potentially not recovering until December 2027. Physical shortages could hit Europe by end of month, particularly in refined products. The EU's release of strategic oil reserves is ongoing, but Dombrovskis noted concerns about shortages in specialized fuels.

The timing compounds Europe's energy transition pressures. With oil prices above $100 per barrel, governments face political pressure to maintain fuel subsidies that contradict decarbonization goals. The Commission has urged member states to ensure support measures are "temporary and targeted" and do not "sustain high demand for fossil fuels."

Japan's core inflation hit a four-year low in April before the energy shock, but analysts expect surging costs in coming months. Asian currencies are also feeling pressure—the region's economies, heavily dependent on Gulf energy imports, face inflationary strain and currency depreciation.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's stagflation warning underscores how the Hormuz closure compounds existing economic stress—constraining fiscal space for clean energy investment while creating political pressure for fossil fuel subsidies that undermine climate targets.

bottomLine: The UAE's OPEC exit, Venezuela's emergence as India's alternative supplier, and Ukraine's deep strikes on Russian refineries all reflect the same underlying dynamic: the Iran conflict is accelerating a fundamental reshaping of global energy relationships. Gulf states are hedging against peak oil demand while maximizing near-term revenues; sanctions-hit producers like Venezuela are finding new markets; and the weaponization of chokepoints—from Hormuz to Malacca to critical minerals—is becoming a standard feature of great power competition. The EU's stagflation warning signals the immediate cost, but the longer-term shift is toward a more fragmented and strategically contested energy map.
sources: Reuters, Al Jazeera, South China Morning Post, OilPrice.com, Foreign Affairs, CNBC, Bloomberg, IEA, Reuters, Observer Research Foundation
